title: Violence
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Violence
summary: Scripture grieves violence as a corruption of God's creation, calls believers to peacemaking, and holds out a vision of a world where swords become plowshares.
body:
Violence enters the biblical story almost immediately. Cain kills Abel, and God confronts the act with a haunting question and a curse [verse:gen.4.10-12]. By Genesis 6, the earth is so filled with violence that God grieves having made humanity at all. Violence is not a minor theme — it is the corruption that most grieves the Creator.

Jesus addresses violence at the level of the heart. He calls his followers to turn the other cheek [verse:matt.5.38-39] — not as passive weakness but as a refusal to let the aggressor set the terms. He rebukes Peter for drawing a sword in the garden: those who live by the sword will die by it [verse:matt.26.52]. His way is different.

Paul extends this ethic to the community: do not repay evil for evil, but overcome evil with good [verse:rom.12.17-21]. The logic is not that evil does not exist but that it is defeated by a superior force — sacrificial love, not retaliatory violence.

The prophetic vision looks toward a world remade. Isaiah pictures a day when nations will beat their swords into plowshares and learn war no more [verse:isa.2.4]. This is not naive optimism — it is hope grounded in the character of a God who will one day make all things new. In the meantime, Jesus declares: blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God [verse:matt.5.9].

verses.annotation: Jesus replaces retaliation with a counter-cultural response — turning the other cheek.
Paul commands overcoming evil with good rather than repaying violence with violence.
The first murder — God holds Cain accountable and the earth itself cries out.
Jesus rebukes the use of the sword — those who live by violence will perish by it.
The prophetic vision of swords beaten into plowshares — the end of war.
Blessed are the peacemakers — they reflect God's character as his children.
The Lord examines the righteous, but his soul hates the one who loves violence.

scholars.quote: Keener reads Jesus' teaching on turning the other cheek not as endorsing passivity but as a creative refusal to participate in the cycle of violence — it asserts dignity while denying the aggressor moral authority.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)

scholars.quote: Moo interprets Romans 12:17-21 as Paul's most sustained argument against retaliatory violence, grounded in trust that God's justice will ultimately prevail and human vengeance is therefore unnecessary.

Oswalt reads Isaiah 2:4 as the ultimate horizon of God's redemptive plan — a world where the instruments of destruction are repurposed for cultivation, because the underlying hostility has been removed.
